formulas.label: Formula 25.1a
formulas.section: DETERMINISTIC SINGLE-LOCUS THEORY
formulas.subsection: Expected Contribution From a Single Locus
formulas.context_text: otypic values of $0:a(1+k):2a$. Assuming genotypes are in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, the contribution to the mean character value from this locus is a function of $p$ (the frequency of $B$), namely, $$ m(p)=2ap\left[1+(1-p)k\right] $$ (25.1a)

formulas.label: Formula 25.1b
formulas.section: DETERMINISTIC SINGLE-LOCUS THEORY
formulas.subsection: Expected Contribution From a Single Locus
formulas.context_text: metic-phase disequilibrium will have no influence on this contribution to the mean. The total contribution from this locus if B is fixed, given that it starts at an initial frequency of $ p_{0} $, is $$ m(1)-m(p_{0})=2a-2ap_{0}\left[1+(1-p_{0})k\right]=2a\left(1-p_{0}\right)(1-p_{0}k) $$ (25.1b)

formulas.label: Formula 25.1c
formulas.section: DETERMINISTIC SINGLE-LOCUS THEORY
formulas.subsection: Expected Contribution From a Single Locus
formulas.context_text: this locus is largest when B is recessive and rare, and smallest when B is dominant and common. With overdominance $ (k > 1) $, the maximum value for $ m(p) $ occurs at at $ p = \widehat{p} $, where $$ \widehat{p}=\frac{1+k}{2k} $$ (25.1c) which is obtained by taking the derivative of $ m(p) $ with respect to p and solving for zero. With an overdominant locus underlying the trait, when $ p_0 > \widehat{p} $, directional selection on th

formulas.label: Formula 25.1d
formulas.section: DETERMINISTIC SINGLE-LOCUS THEORY
formulas.subsection: Expected Contribution From a Single Locus
formulas.context_text: ional selection on the trait results in p decreasing to $ \widehat{p} $, while if $ p_0 < \widehat{p} $, then p increases to $ \widehat{p} $. In either case, the final contribution from this locus is $$ m(\widehat{p})-m(p_{0}) $$ (25.1d)

formulas.label: Formula 25.1e
formulas.section: DETERMINISTIC SINGLE-LOCUS THEORY
formulas.subsection: Expected Contribution From a Single Locus
formulas.context_text: Finally, the allele frequency, $ p_{\beta} $, at which a preset fraction, $ \beta $, of the total contribution occurs is also of interest. This is determined by solving the quadratic equation $$ m(p_{\beta})-m(p_{0})=\beta\left[m(1)-m(p_{0})\right] $$ (25.1e)

formulas.label: Formula 25.2a
formulas.section: DETERMINISTIC SINGLE-LOCUS THEORY
formulas.subsection: Dudley's Estimators of a, n, and $ p_{0} $
formulas.context_text: e starting additive variance is $ 2na^2p_0(1 - p_0) $. Using these expressions yields Robertson's (1970a) result for the total response, scaled by the square root of the initial additive variance, as $$ \begin{align*}{R\over\sigma_A}={2na(1-p_0)\over\sqrt{2na^2p_0(1-p_0)}}=\sqrt{2n(1-p_0)\over p_0}\end{align*} $$ (25.2a)

formulas.label: Formula 25.2b
formulas.section: DETERMINISTIC SINGLE-LOCUS THEORY
formulas.subsection: Dudley's Estimators of a, n, and $ p_{0} $
formulas.context_text: Dudley (1977) noted that, in a divergence selection experiment (Chapter 18), Equation 25.2a yields the limit, $ R_{H} $, for response in the high direction, while $$ \begin{align*}{R_L\over\sigma_A}={2nap_0\over\sqrt{2na^2p_0(1-p_0)}}=\sqrt{{2np_0\over1-p_0}}\end{align*} $$ (25.2b) yields the limit to response in the low direction. Taking the ratio of these two limits yields $$ \frac{R_{H}}{R_{L}}=\frac{\sqrt{2n(1-p_{0})/p_{0}}}{\sqrt{2np_{0}/(1-p_{0})}}=\frac{1-p_{0}}{p_{0}} $

formulas.label: Formula 25.2c
formulas.section: DETERMINISTIC SINGLE-LOCUS THEORY
formulas.subsection: Dudley's Estimators of a, n, and $ p_{0} $
formulas.context_text: *}{R_L\over\sigma_A}={2nap_0\over\sqrt{2na^2p_0(1-p_0)}}=\sqrt{{2np_0\over1-p_0}}\end{align*} $$ (25.2b) yields the limit to response in the low direction. Taking the ratio of these two limits yields $$ \frac{R_{H}}{R_{L}}=\frac{\sqrt{2n(1-p_{0})/p_{0}}}{\sqrt{2np_{0}/(1-p_{0})}}=\frac{1-p_{0}}{p_{0}} $$ (25.2c)

formulas.label: Formula 25.2d

formulas.subsection: Dudley's Estimators of a, n, and $ p_{0} $
formulas.context_text: Equation 25.2c rearranges to suggest an estimate of $ p_{0} $, with $$ \widehat{p}_{0}=\frac{1}{R_{H}/R_{L}+1} $$ (25.2d)

formulas.label: Formula 25.2e
formulas.section: DETERMINISTIC SINGLE-LOCUS THEORY
formulas.subsection: Dudley's Estimators of a, n, and $ p_{0} $
formulas.context_text: Next, because $ R_H R_L = 4n^2 a^2 p_0 (1 - p_0) $, it follows that $ R_H R_L / \sigma_A^2 = 2n $, which suggests an estimator for the number of loci $$ \widehat{n}=\frac{1}{2}\frac{R_{H}R_{L}}{\sigma_{A}^{2}} $$ (25.2e)

formulas.label: Formula 25.2f
formulas.section: DETERMINISTIC SINGLE-LOCUS THEORY
formulas.subsection: Dudley's Estimators of a, n, and $ p_{0} $
formulas.context_text: Finally, a little algebra recovers an estimate of the allelic effect, namely, $$ \widehat{a}=\sigma_{A}^{2}\left(\frac{1}{R_{H}}+\frac{1}{R_{L}}\right) $$ (25.2f)

Setting Equation 25.17d equal to zero and solving for the limiting mean yields $$ \mu_{\infty}=\bar{\imath}\omega\frac{\sqrt{\sigma_{z}^{2}+\omega^{2}}}{\sigma_{z}} $$ (25.18a) or a total response of $ \mu_{\infty} - \mu_0 $, a result first obtained by James (1962). Note that this limit does not appear to depend on $ h^2 $ (provided it is nonzero at the limit). However, thi

formulas.label: Formula 25.18b
formulas.section: CONFLICTS BETWEEN NATURAL AND ARTIFICIAL SELECTION
formulas.subsection: Artificial Selection Countered by Natural Stabilizing Selection
formulas.context_text: variation). If the initial mean is zero ( $ \mu_0 = 0 $, i.e., the population mean starts at the optimum), then recalling Equation 25.17d yields a response after an initial generation of selection of $$ R_{1}=h^{2}S=h^{2}\left(\frac{\overline{\imath}\sigma_{z}\omega}{\sqrt{\sigma_{z}^{2}+\omega^{2}}}\right) $$ (25.18b) returning the ratio of the total to initial response as $$ \frac{R_{c}(\infty)}{R_{1}}=\frac{\sigma_{z}^{2}+\omega^{2}}{\sigma_{z}^{2}h^{2}}=\frac{\sigma_{z}^{2}+\omega^{2}}{\sigma_{A}^{2}} $$ (25.18

formulas.label: Formula 25.18c
formulas.section: CONFLICTS BETWEEN NATURAL AND ARTIFICIAL SELECTION
formulas.subsection: Artificial Selection Countered by Natural Stabilizing Selection
formulas.context_text: ration of selection of $$ R_{1}=h^{2}S=h^{2}\left(\frac{\overline{\imath}\sigma_{z}\omega}{\sqrt{\sigma_{z}^{2}+\omega^{2}}}\right) $$ (25.18b) returning the ratio of the total to initial response as $$ \frac{R_{c}(\infty)}{R_{1}}=\frac{\sigma_{z}^{2}+\omega^{2}}{\sigma_{z}^{2}h^{2}}=\frac{\sigma_{z}^{2}+\omega^{2}}{\sigma_{A}^{2}} $$ (25.18c)

formulas.label: Formula 25.18d
formulas.section: CONFLICTS BETWEEN NATURAL AND ARTIFICIAL SELECTION
formulas.subsection: Artificial Selection Countered by Natural Stabilizing Selection
formulas.context_text: The half-life of response (in generations) is $$ t_{0.5}=\ln(2)\frac{\sigma_{z}^{2}+\omega^{2}}{\sigma_{z}^{2}h^{2}} $$ (25.18d) as found by James (1962) and Zeng and Hill (1986).
